planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after cold stratification in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; western larch is one of the fastest-growing conifers of the Pacific Northwest, requiring full sun and well-drained, mineral soils. Does not tolerate shade, drought, or poorly drained conditions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; western larch is the largest and most commercially important of the North American larches, reaching 100–180 feet in the forests of the inland Pacific Northwest. A deciduous conifer — like all larches it drops its needles in fall after they turn a brilliant, luminous golden-yellow, one of the most spectacular fall displays in the western mountains. The needles grow in characteristic larch clusters of 14–30 needles from woody spur shoots on older wood, with single needles on the leading growth. Western larch wood is the strongest and most rot-resistant of the western conifers, used for structural lumber, railroad ties, and telephone poles. The thick, fire-resistant bark protects old trees from low-intensity forest fires. A magnificent landscape tree where space allows, providing the rare combination of exceptional scale, distinctive form, and spectacular fall color.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after cold stratification in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; eastern larch grows naturally in cold bogs, wet forest edges, and well-drained upland soils across the boreal zone. One of the most cold-hardy trees in North America — tolerating temperatures to -65°F in its northern range.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; eastern larch (tamarack) is the hardiest of North American conifers, native to the boreal forests from Labrador to Alaska and south to northern Minnesota, growing even at the edge of the Arctic tundra. Like all larches it is a deciduous conifer — the soft, blue-green needles emerge in clusters in spring, providing a feathery appearance, then turn a brilliant golden yellow in fall before dropping, producing a spectacular effect unusual among conifers. In bog habitats, tamarack grows in pure stands or with black spruce. The small cones persist on the tree for several years. The flexible, rot-resistant wood was used by many northern indigenous peoples for snowshoe frames, basket ribs, and boat ribs. Excellent for bog gardens, moist areas, and extremely cold climates in zones 1–6 where few other conifers are adapted.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after cold stratification in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; European larch is one of the fastest-growing conifers in European forestry, particularly in the mountains. It requires full sun and good drainage but is tolerant of cold, snow, and wind.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, moist to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; European larch is the dominant conifer of the Alps, Carpathians, and Sudeten mountains — the classic 'golden mountain' tree whose needles turn brilliant gold in October above the treeline, creating the famous autumn color displays in the Swiss and Austrian Alps. A fast-growing, long-lived, graceful tree producing the characteristic clustered needles on spur shoots of all larches — soft, light green in spring, deepening to rich green in summer, then golden-yellow in fall. The small, oval cones are among the most attractive of any conifer. Widely used in British and European forestry and ornamental planting. The weeping cultivar 'Pendula' is a dramatic, cascading accent tree. More adaptable to garden conditions than western larch, growing in a wider range of well-drained soils in zones 2–7.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Monterey pine is native to only three small coastal areas in California and is tender to hard frost, but grows extremely rapidly in mild coastal climates where it is widely used as a windbreak and timber tree.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Monterey pine is paradoxically an extremely rare native tree in the US — naturally occurring in just three small, fog-influenced coastal populations near Monterey, California, Santa Cruz Island, and Cedros Island in Baja California — yet it is the most widely planted timber tree in the Southern Hemisphere, with vast plantations in New Zealand, Australia, Chile, South Africa, and Spain. The species grows extraordinarily fast in moist, mild coastal climates — 6–10 feet per year is common. The cones are serotinous (requiring heat to open) and often remain on the tree for decades, making fire an important part of the species' ecology. In California coastal gardens, it provides fast shade and wind protection. Despite its global importance as a timber tree, the native Monterey pine populations are listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to disease, drought stress, and habitat loss.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after cold stratification in very well-drained, rocky, lean, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, dolomitic or limestone-derived soil in full sun; Great Basin bristlecone pine grows naturally on exposed, rocky, dolomitic ridges at 9,000–11,500 feet elevation in the White Mountains and Great Basin ranges. Requires excellent drainage and lean, nutrient-poor soils — very fertile conditions cause early death.

directions: Plant seeds in spring in very well-drained, rocky, lean, slightly alkaline, dolomitic or sandy soil in full sun; Great Basin bristlecone pine is the world's oldest known living organism — individual trees in the White Mountains of California and the mountains of Nevada exceed 5,000 years of age. Methuselah, a named specimen in the White Mountains, is over 4,800 years old. The trees survive extreme conditions of thin dolomitic soil, blistering cold, intense UV radiation, and annual drought that kill competitors, and their resinous, dense wood resists decay for thousands of years after death. In garden culture, bristlecone pines are surprisingly adaptable to lower elevations if given lean, extremely well-drained soil and full sun; they grow very slowly (adding only a few inches per year), making them perfect long-term bonsai subjects or rock garden specimens. The distinctive bottle-brush branch tips (with dense needle clusters on spur shoots) and the distinctive bristly cones are unmistakable. A tree grown from seed will still be young in several human lifetimes.
